era_context: Early 16th century: the High Renaissance in Italy; the Portuguese maritime empire expanding under King Manuel I; the Ottoman Empire consolidating power under Bayezid II; the Ming Dynasty in China under the Hongwu Emperor's successors; the Safavid Empire in Persia under Ismail I; the Spanish Reconquista completed, with Spain consolidating colonial holdings in the Americas and North Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal contacts beginning)
Interior of the Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary; Spanish conquest narratives dominate)

events.title: Columbus's fourth and final voyage begins; exploration of Central American coast
events.description: Christopher Columbus departed Spain on 11 May 1502 on his fourth and final voyage to the Americas. This voyage would last until 1504 and would explore the coasts of present-day Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama. Columbus was searching for a westward passage to the Indian Ocean, which he believed existed. The voyage was marked by severe storms, shipworm damage to his vessels, and increasing conflict with indigenous peoples. Columbus's health deteriorated during the voyage, and he would return to Spain in poor condition in 1504. This voyage marked the effective end of Columbus's exploration career and the beginning of systematic Spanish colonization under other leaders.

sources.name: Ferdinand Columbus, Life of the Admiral

events.certainty_note: Columbus's departure date for his fourth voyage is well-documented in multiple sources.

events.title: Ismail I founds the Safavid Empire; declaration of Twelver Shi'ism as state religion
events.description: Ismail I (r. 1501–1524) founded the Safavid Empire in 1501 and by 1502 had consolidated his control over much of Persia. He declared Twelver Shi'ism the state religion, marking a major shift in the religious landscape of the Middle East. The Safavid state represented a Shi'ite counterweight to the Sunni Ottoman and Mamluk powers. Ismail I was a charismatic military leader who commanded the loyalty of the Qizilbash, a military elite devoted to his person. By 1502, the Safavid Empire was expanding rapidly and would soon come into conflict with the Ottoman Empire.

sources.name: Safavid court chronicles and later Persian historiography

events.certainty_note: Ismail I's founding of the Safavid Empire in 1501 and his declaration of Twelver Shi'ism as the state religion are attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: 1514-001 (Battle of Chaldiran between Safavids and Ottomans)

events.title: Leonardo da Vinci returns to Florence; begins work on major projects
events.description: Leonardo da Vinci returned to Florence in 1502 after spending several years in Milan and other cities. In Florence, he was commissioned to paint the Battle of Anghiari for the Palazzo della Signoria, a major public commission. He also worked on various other projects, including studies for paintings and inventions. By 1502, Leonardo was at the height of his creative powers and was recognized as one of the greatest artists and thinkers of the Renaissance. His influence on contemporary and subsequent artists was profound. However, many of his projects remained unfinished due to his perfectionism and diverse interests.
events.certainty_note: Leonardo's return to Florence and his commission for the Battle of Anghiari are documented in contemporary records.
cross_references: 1504-003 (Leonardo continues work on projects in Florence)

events.title: Montezuma II becomes Aztec emperor; consolidation of Aztec power
events.description: Montezuma II (also known as Moctezuma II) became the ninth emperor of the Aztec Empire in 1502, following the death of his uncle Ahuitzotl. Montezuma II was an able military commander and administrator who consolidated Aztec power and expanded the empire's tributary system. He was known for his piety and his support of the priesthood. However, his reign would be cut short by the arrival of Spanish conquistadors under Hernán Cortés in 1519. Montezuma II's initial response to the Spanish was cautious and diplomatic, which some historians have interpreted as a failure to recognize the existential threat posed by the Spanish conquest.

sources.name: Aztec codices and indigenous accounts (recorded after Spanish conquest)
Spanish conquest narratives by Bernal Díaz del Castillo and others

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's accession in 1502 is attested in Aztec codices and Spanish conquest narratives, though the exact date may vary slightly depending on the calendar system used.

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post at Mozambique; expansion of Portuguese control in East Africa
events.description: Portuguese forces established a fortified trading post at Mozambique in 1502 as part of their broader strategy to monopolize the Indian Ocean spice trade. The establishment of Mozambique as a Portuguese base marked a crucial step in Portuguese control over East African coastal trade routes. The Portuguese used military force to establish their presence, displacing Arab and Swahili merchant networks that had dominated the region for centuries. Mozambique would remain a major Portuguese possession in Africa until 1975.
key_figures: Vasco da Gama (Portuguese explorer and commander)

sources.name: Portuguese royal archives and contemporary dispatches

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Portuguese trading post at Mozambique in 1502 is well-documented in Portuguese sources.
cross_references: 1498-001 (Vasco da Gama's first voyage to India)

events.title: Bayezid II consolidates Ottoman power; Ottoman Empire at height of territorial expansion
events.description: Bayezid II (r. 1481–1512) was consolidating Ottoman power in 1502. He had ruled for over 20 years and had expanded Ottoman territory significantly, including the conquest of Constantinople in 1453 (under his predecessor Mehmed II) and subsequent expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia. By 1502, the Ottoman Empire was at the height of its territorial expansion and was recognized as a major power in the Mediterranean and Eastern Europe. However, Bayezid II was aging and would be succeeded by his son Selim I in 1512, who would further expand Ottoman power.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid II's reign and the Ottoman Empire's territorial expansion are well-documented in Ottoman sources.

disconfirming_evidence: 1. Montezuma II's accession date is sometimes given as 1502 or 1503, depending on the calendar system used (Aztec vs. Gregorian). Modern scholarship generally accepts 1502 as the date. 2. The exact date of Portuguese establishment of trading posts in East Africa (Mozambique, Sofala) is sometimes uncertain, with sources varying on whether specific posts were established in 1502, 1503, or 1504. 3. Leonardo da Vinci's return to Florence is sometimes dated to 1501 rather than 1502; the exact date is uncertain, but he was definitely in Florence by 1502.
historiographic_note: The year 1502 is well-documented in European sources (Portuguese maritime expansion, Italian Renaissance, Spanish colonial affairs) and Ottoman sources (Ottoman consolidation of power). Chinese sources (Ming court records) provide detailed records of court politics. Safavid sources are less detailed for this specific year. Aztec sources (codices) are fragmentary and were recorded after the Spanish conquest, making them subject to Spanish influence. Sub-Saharan African and interior American sources are minimal; Portuguese and Spanish colonial narratives dominate. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and cultural events in Europe, the Mediterranean, Asia, and Mesoamerica; economic and social history of non-elite populations is underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Columbus's fourth voyage, which began in 1502, ended in 1504 with his return to Spain in poor health.
Ismail I's founding of the Safavid Empire in 1501–1502 led to conflict with the Ottoman Empire, culminating in the Battle of Chaldiran in 1514.
Montezuma II's accession in 1502 led to his reign as Aztec emperor, which would be cut short by the arrival of Spanish conquistadors in 1519.
Portuguese establishment of trading posts in East Africa in 1502 was part of the broader strategy of Portuguese monopolization of Indian Ocean trade.
